name: push_to_set_superset
reason: The pushed sequence's set is always a superset of the original — this is correct but means push can never shrink the set, which could mask bugs where callers expect set membership to be revocable via sequence operations

name: push_duplicate_set_unchanged
reason: Pushing a duplicate element leaves the set unchanged — the set view loses information about sequence length, so two sequences of different lengths can have identical set views, hiding multiplicity

name: push_to_set_contains_all_original
reason: All original elements survive in the pushed set — this is expected but combined with the lemma could let callers assume containment persists across arbitrary push chains without re-checking

name: push_empty_singleton_set
reason: Pushing onto empty yields a singleton set — trivially true but confirms the base case; the lemma provides no cardinality guarantee (the set could still appear to have size 0 or >1 in buggy set axioms)

name: push_to_set_len_not_preserved
reason: Sequence length grows by 1 but set view stays the same — the disconnect between sequence length and set cardinality means the `to_set()` abstraction irreversibly loses multiplicity information after this lemma is applied
